articles.headline: India Unveils Phase II of National Green Hydrogen Grid Mission, Targets 20 GW Capacity by 2030
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) launched Phase II of the National Green Hydrogen Grid Mission, focusing on scaling up production and establishing dedicated green hydrogen corridors.
• This phase aims to achieve 20 GW of electrolyser capacity and 5 million metric tonnes (MMT) annual green hydrogen production by 2030, a significant increase from initial targets.
• Key components include developing dedicated infrastructure for storage, transport, and dispensing of green hydrogen, alongside incentives for industrial adoption in sectors like refining, fertilisers, and steel.
• The mission emphasizes 'Make in India' for electrolyser manufacturing and R&D in advanced technologies, aiming for cost reduction and export capabilities.
• Financial outlays include Production Linked Incentives (PLI) for manufacturing and viability gap funding for early projects, bolstering India's energy transition goals.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy
Environmental Pollution and Degradation
Conservation
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission launched in 2023 with an initial outlay of ₹19,744 crore.
Targeted production of 5 MMT of green hydrogen by 2030.
Green hydrogen produced using renewable energy through electrolysis.
SIGHT (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition) Programme is a key component.
Applications include fertilisers, refineries, steel, cement, and mobility.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's strategy for achieving energy independence and meeting climate commitments through green hydrogen.
Analyze the economic potential and challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production and adoption.
Examine the role of policy incentives (PLI, VGF) and infrastructure development in driving the green hydrogen economy.
Evaluate the geopolitical implications of India becoming a major player in the global green hydrogen market.
Address environmental benefits such as reduced carbon emissions and air pollution.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of a green hydrogen production facility with wind turbines and solar panels in the background.
articles.trivia: India's first green hydrogen blending operation in the piped natural gas (PNG) network was successfully commissioned in Gujarat.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Streamlines Guidelines for 'Living Will' and Passive Euthanasia
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court issued revised guidelines to simplify the procedure for executing and implementing 'Living Wills' for passive euthanasia, addressing practical difficulties faced since its 2018 judgment.
• The new guidelines aim to make the process more accessible and less cumbersome, reducing bureaucratic hurdles and ensuring timely decisions in critical medical situations.
• Key changes include streamlining the role of medical boards, clarifying the involvement of family members, and emphasizing the patient's right to dignity in death.
• The court reiterated the importance of an Advance Medical Directive (Living Will) as an expression of an individual's right to self-determination and bodily autonomy under Article 21.
• This move is expected to provide greater clarity for both patients and healthcare providers, ensuring that end-of-life decisions are respected.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Judiciary
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court recognized 'Living Will' in 2018 in Common Cause vs Union of India case.
Article 21 (Right to Life) includes the right to die with dignity.
Passive euthanasia involves withdrawing life support, while active euthanasia involves administering lethal substances.
The new guidelines simplify the certification process by primary and secondary medical boards.
The court's ruling mandates that the 'Living Will' be attested by a notary or gazetted officer and two independent witnesses.
mainsPoints: Analyze the ethical and legal complexities surrounding the 'Right to Die with Dignity' in India.
Discuss the challenges in implementing Advance Medical Directives in a diverse socio-cultural context like India.
Critically evaluate the Supreme Court's role in balancing individual autonomy with societal concerns regarding end-of-life decisions.
Examine the implications of these guidelines for healthcare professionals and the healthcare system.
Suggest further reforms needed in palliative care and end-of-life support systems.
articles.imageDescription: A gavel resting on a legal book, symbolizing a Supreme Court judgment.
articles.trivia: The Netherlands was the first country to legalize euthanasia in 2002.

articles.headline: India-EFTA Trade and Economic Partnership Agreement (TEPA) Nears Ratification
articles.explainer:
• The India-EFTA Trade and Economic Partnership Agreement (TEPA) is reportedly in its final stages of ratification, signaling a major boost for India's trade relations with European Free Trade Association (EFTA) member states.
• TEPA aims to reduce tariffs, enhance market access for goods and services, and promote investment between India and EFTA countries (Switzerland, Norway, Iceland, and Liechtenstein).
• India is expected to gain significant access to European markets for its services sector, particularly IT, while EFTA countries will benefit from reduced duties on industrial goods and technology transfer.
• The agreement includes commitments on Intellectual Property Rights (IPR), sustainable development, and dispute settlement mechanisms, fostering a comprehensive economic partnership.
• Ratification of TEPA is seen as a strategic move to diversify India's trade partners and integrate further into global supply chains amidst evolving geopolitical landscapes.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: EFTA members: Switzerland, Norway, Iceland, Liechtenstein.
TEPA is a comprehensive agreement covering trade in goods, services, investment, IPR, and sustainable development.
India's key exports to EFTA include chemicals, textiles, and precious stones.
India's key imports from EFTA include machinery, pharmaceuticals, and chemicals.
The agreement includes a legally binding commitment to promote investments and job creation.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic significance of the India-EFTA TEPA for India's foreign trade policy and economic growth.
Discuss the potential benefits and challenges for key Indian sectors, particularly services and manufacturing, from this agreement.
Evaluate how TEPA aligns with India's broader strategy of diversifying trade partnerships and integrating into global value chains.
Examine the role of IPR and sustainability clauses in modern trade agreements like TEPA.
Compare TEPA with other FTAs India has signed or is negotiating, highlighting its unique features.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and EFTA member countries (Switzerland, Norway, Iceland, Liechtenstein) arranged together.
articles.trivia: EFTA was established in 1960 as an alternative to the European Economic Community (EEC).

articles.headline: ISRO's Shukrayaan-1 Successfully Enters Venus Orbit, Begins Scientific Operations
articles.explainer:
• India's maiden mission to Venus, Shukrayaan-1, successfully executed its Venus Orbit Insertion (VOI) maneuver, placing the spacecraft into the designated elliptical orbit around the planet.
• The spacecraft, equipped with a comprehensive suite of scientific instruments, has commenced its primary mission of studying Venus's atmosphere, surface, and subsurface.
• Key objectives include understanding the planet's atmospheric composition and dynamics, investigating its surface features and volcanism, and exploring the interaction of solar wind with Venusian ionosphere.
• Data from Shukrayaan-1 is expected to provide crucial insights into Venus's geological history, climate evolution, and habitability potential, contributing to planetary science.
• This achievement marks a significant milestone for ISRO, further solidifying India's capabilities in interplanetary exploration.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: Shukrayaan-1 is ISRO's first mission to Venus.
It carries a Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) and an atmospheric spectrometer.
Venus is often called Earth's 'sister planet' due to similar size and mass.
Venus has a thick, toxic atmosphere of carbon dioxide and sulfuric acid clouds.
The mission aims to study Venus's 'super-rotation' of its atmosphere.
mainsPoints: Discuss the scientific objectives and potential contributions of ISRO's Shukrayaan-1 mission to planetary science.
Analyze India's growing capabilities in space exploration and its implications for global scientific collaboration.
Examine the technological challenges involved in designing and executing an interplanetary mission to Venus.
Evaluate the broader significance of such missions for inspiring scientific temper and fostering innovation in India.
Compare Shukrayaan-1 with previous international missions to Venus and highlight its unique aspects.
articles.imageDescription: Artistic rendition of the Shukrayaan-1 spacecraft orbiting Venus, with Venus's cloudy atmosphere visible.
articles.trivia: Venus rotates in the opposite direction to most planets in our solar system, a phenomenon called retrograde rotation.

articles.headline: Government Launches Enhanced National Wetland Conservation Plan, Focus on Ramsar Sites
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) unveiled an enhanced National Wetland Conservation Plan (NWCP) with a renewed focus on the protection and sustainable management of Ramsar Sites.
• The updated plan integrates climate change adaptation strategies, community participation, and data-driven management tools to address threats like pollution, encroachment, and hydrological alterations.
• Special emphasis is placed on developing site-specific management plans for each Ramsar site, ensuring ecological character is maintained and ecosystem services are preserved.
• Funding mechanisms and capacity building initiatives for state wetland authorities and local communities are key pillars of the revised strategy.
• The move aims to strengthen India's commitment to the Ramsar Convention and enhance biodiversity conservation efforts across the country.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
prelimsPoints: Ramsar Convention (Convention on Wetlands) signed in 1971 in Ramsar, Iran.
India is a signatory to the Ramsar Convention since 1982.
Wetlands are vital for water purification, flood control, and biodiversity.
Montreux Record is a register of Ramsar sites where ecological character has changed or is likely to change.
India has a significant number of Ramsar Sites (e.g., Sundarbans, Chilika Lake, Loktak Lake).
mainsPoints: Discuss the ecological and economic significance of wetlands and the threats they face in India.
Evaluate the effectiveness of previous wetland conservation efforts and the need for an enhanced NWCP.
Analyze how community participation and climate change adaptation can be integrated into wetland management strategies.
Examine the challenges in implementing site-specific management plans for Ramsar sites across diverse regions.
Suggest policy recommendations for strengthening legal frameworks and enforcement for wetland protection.
articles.imageDescription: A serene wetland landscape with migratory birds and lush vegetation, highlighting biodiversity.
articles.trivia: Chilika Lake in Odisha was the first Indian wetland of international importance designated under the Ramsar Convention.

articles.headline: RBI Mandates Strict Compliance with Revised Digital Lending Guidelines for NBFCs and Banks
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) issued a directive emphasizing strict compliance with its revised Digital Lending Guidelines, particularly for Non-Banking Financial Companies (NBFCs) and banks.
• The guidelines aim to protect borrowers from predatory lending practices, unfair recovery methods, and data privacy breaches prevalent in the digital lending ecosystem.
• Key provisions include direct disbursement of loans to borrowers' accounts, disclosure of all charges, a cooling-off period, and restrictions on automatic credit limit increases.
• The RBI has warned of stringent action against entities failing to adhere to the regulations, reinforcing its commitment to fostering a safe and transparent digital lending environment.
• This move is crucial for enhancing consumer confidence in digital financial services and curbing the proliferation of illegal lending apps.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization, of resources, growth, development and employment.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: RBI released 'Guidelines on Digital Lending' in September 2022.
The guidelines cover regulated entities (REs) and their Lending Service Providers (LSPs).
Key focus areas: transparency, fair practices, and data security.
First Loan Default Guarantee (FLDG) arrangements are also under RBI scrutiny.
A 'digital lending app' (DLA) is an app operated by an RE or LSP.
mainsPoints: Analyze the challenges and opportunities presented by the rapid growth of digital lending in India.
Discuss how RBI's digital lending guidelines aim to balance innovation with consumer protection and financial stability.

Suggest measures to further enhance financial literacy and awareness among digital borrowers.

articles.headline: Integrated Theatre Commands: Progress and Challenges in India's Military Reorganization
articles.explainer:
• India's ambitious plan to establish Integrated Theatre Commands (ITCs) is moving forward, with significant progress made in identifying command structures and operational doctrines.
• The ITCs aim to integrate the assets and personnel of the Army, Navy, and Air Force under a single commander for specific geographical or functional areas, enhancing synergy and combat effectiveness.
• Challenges remain in overcoming inter-service rivalries, optimizing resource allocation, and standardizing logistics and communication systems across the three services.
• The Department of Military Affairs (DMA) and the Chief of Defence Staff (CDS) are spearheading the reforms, emphasizing jointness and a unified approach to national security.
• Successful implementation of ITCs is crucial for India to effectively counter multi-domain threats and project power in the evolving geopolitical landscape.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
prelimsPoints: Chief of Defence Staff (CDS) is the principal military advisor to the Defence Minister.
CDS is also the Permanent Chairman of the Chiefs of Staff Committee (COSC).
The aim of ITCs is to achieve synergy in operations and logistics among the three services.
Currently, India has 17 single-service commands.
Potential theatre commands include Northern, Western, Peninsular, and Air Defence Command.
mainsPoints: Discuss the rationale behind the creation of Integrated Theatre Commands and their potential benefits for India's national security.
Analyze the major challenges and hurdles in the implementation of military theatre commands in India.
Evaluate the role of the Chief of Defence Staff (CDS) and the Department of Military Affairs (DMA) in driving these reforms.
Examine how ITCs can enhance India's response to emerging multi-domain threats and regional conflicts.
Compare India's theatre command model with similar structures in other major military powers.
articles.imageDescription: Three uniformed personnel representing Army, Navy, and Air Force standing together, symbolizing jointness.
articles.trivia: The concept of integrated theatre commands was first recommended by the Kargil Review Committee in 1999.

articles.headline: National Education Policy 2020: Implementation Challenges in Higher Education Sector
articles.explainer:
• A recent report highlights significant challenges in the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, particularly concerning the higher education sector.
• Issues include inadequate funding, shortage of qualified faculty, resistance to curriculum reforms, and disparities in infrastructure across institutions.
• The multi-disciplinary approach and flexible credit system, while transformative, require substantial investment in faculty training and institutional capacity building.
• Stakeholder consultations reveal concerns about the pace of change and the need for greater clarity on regulatory frameworks and accreditation processes.
• Successful implementation of NEP 2020 is critical for transforming India into a global knowledge superpower and addressing skill gaps in the workforce.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the National Policy on Education of 1986.
Aims to increase GER in higher education to 50% by 2035.
Proposes a four-year multidisciplinary Bachelor's degree with multiple exit options.
National Research Foundation (NRF) is proposed to fund research.
Emphasis on foundational literacy and numeracy in school education.
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative potential of NEP 2020 in reforming India's higher education system.
Analyze the major challenges hindering the effective implementation of NEP 2020 in universities and colleges.
Evaluate the role of government funding, private sector investment, and institutional autonomy in achieving NEP's goals.
Examine how the proposed multidisciplinary approach and flexible credit system can address skill gaps and enhance employability.

articles.headline: Government Proposes Amendments to PMLA to Broaden Scope, Include New Offenses
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Finance is reportedly working on amendments to the Prevention of Money Laundering Act (PMLA), 2002, to broaden its scope and include new categories of offenses.
• The proposed changes aim to strengthen India's legal framework against financial crimes, bring it in line with international standards set by the Financial Action Task Force (FATF), and tackle emerging forms of money laundering.
• New offenses under consideration include certain types of cyber fraud, illegal wildlife trade financing, and specific tax evasion schemes.
• The amendments also seek to enhance the powers of the Enforcement Directorate (ED) while ensuring safeguards against misuse.
• This move reflects India's commitment to combating illicit financial flows and improving its global standing in anti-money laundering efforts.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Role of civil services in a democracy.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
prelimsPoints: PMLA was enacted in 2002 to combat money laundering and confiscate property derived from it.
Enforcement Directorate (ED) is responsible for enforcing PMLA.
India is a member of the FATF.
FATF's recommendations are international standards to combat money laundering and terrorist financing.
Section 3 of PMLA defines the offense of money laundering.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolution and significance of the PMLA in combating financial crimes in India.
Analyze the rationale behind the proposed amendments to PMLA and their alignment with international standards.
Evaluate the balance between strengthening enforcement powers and ensuring protection against potential misuse of PMLA provisions.
Examine the challenges in tackling new forms of money laundering, such as those involving cryptocurrencies and cyber fraud.
Suggest measures to enhance inter-agency coordination and capacity building for effective PMLA enforcement.
articles.imageDescription: A stack of currency notes with a magnifying glass over it, symbolizing financial scrutiny.
articles.trivia: The term 'money laundering' originated from the practice of mafia groups in the US using laundromats to mix illegally obtained cash with legitimate income.

articles.headline: India Actively Engages in COP29 Preparatory Talks, Emphasizes Climate Finance for Developing Nations
articles.explainer:
• India is actively participating in preparatory discussions for the upcoming COP29 (Conference of the Parties) in Azerbaijan, strongly advocating for enhanced climate finance for developing countries.
• India's stance highlights the need for developed nations to meet their commitment of $100 billion annually and to establish a new, higher collective quantified goal (NCQG) for post-2025 climate finance.
• Discussions focus on adaptation finance, loss and damage mechanisms, and ensuring equitable burden-sharing in global climate action.
• India also stressed the importance of technology transfer and capacity building to support developing economies in their transition to green pathways.
• The preparatory talks are crucial for shaping the agenda and potential outcomes of COP29, particularly regarding financial support and implementation mechanisms.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
keyTerms: United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC)
prelimsPoints: COP29 is scheduled to be held in Baku, Azerbaijan.
UNFCCC was signed at the Earth Summit in Rio de Janeiro in 1992.
The $100 billion climate finance goal was set at COP15 in Copenhagen (2009).
Loss and Damage fund was operationalized at COP28 in Dubai.
India's NDCs aim to reduce emissions intensity of its GDP by 45% by 2030 from 2005 level.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's negotiating position on climate finance and its significance for global climate justice.
Analyze the challenges in mobilizing and delivering climate finance to developing countries, particularly the $100 billion goal.
Evaluate the concept of 'Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities' (CBDR-RC) in the context of global climate action.
Examine the role of technology transfer and capacity building in enabling developing nations to achieve their climate targets.
Suggest mechanisms to ensure transparency and accountability in climate finance flows and their utilization.

articles.trivia: The Paris Agreement (2015) aims to limit global warming to well below 2, preferably to 1.5 degrees Celsius, compared to pre-industrial levels.

articles.headline: Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY): Performance Review and Proposed Reforms
articles.explainer:
• A comprehensive review of the Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) highlighted its successes in providing crop insurance to farmers but also identified areas for improvement.
• Key issues include delays in claim settlement, challenges in accurate yield assessment, and limited coverage for certain localized risks and non-loanee farmers.
• Proposed reforms focus on leveraging technology for faster claim processing (e.g., satellite imagery, drones), enhancing transparency, and increasing farmer awareness about the scheme's benefits.
• The government aims to make PMFBY more farmer-friendly and efficient, ensuring timely financial support to farmers affected by crop losses due to natural calamities.
• Strengthening the scheme is vital for ensuring agricultural resilience and farmer welfare, especially in the context of climate change impacts.
gsSubTopics: Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System— objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
prelimsPoints: PMFBY launched in 2016, replacing National Agricultural Insurance Scheme (NAIS) and Modified NAIS.
Compulsory for loanee farmers, voluntary for others.
Farmers pay a uniform premium of 2% for Kharif crops, 1.5% for Rabi crops, and 5% for commercial/horticultural crops.
The scheme is administered by the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare.
Covers yield losses due to non-preventable natural risks, including localized risks and post-harvest losses.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and impact of PMFBY in providing risk coverage to Indian farmers.
Analyze the key challenges faced in the implementation of PMFBY and its effectiveness in mitigating farmer distress.
Evaluate the role of technology (drones, satellite imagery) in improving the accuracy of crop cutting experiments and claim settlement.
Examine the need for reforms to ensure greater inclusivity and transparency in the scheme, particularly for non-loanee farmers.
Suggest policy measures to make agricultural insurance more accessible, affordable, and responsive to the needs of diverse farming communities.
articles.imageDescription: A farmer inspecting damaged crops in a field, symbolizing crop loss and insurance need.
articles.trivia: The average size of landholding in India is around 1.08 hectares (2015-16 Agriculture Census).

articles.headline: Government Initiates Consultations on Ethical and Regulatory Framework for AI in Healthcare
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, in collaboration with NITI Aayog, has initiated multi-stakeholder consultations to develop an ethical and regulatory framework for Artificial Intelligence (AI) in healthcare.
• The discussions address critical concerns such as data privacy, algorithmic bias, accountability for AI-driven medical decisions, and equitable access to AI-powered healthcare solutions.
• The framework aims to foster innovation in AI applications while ensuring patient safety, maintaining ethical standards, and preventing discriminatory outcomes.
• Experts emphasize the need for a robust legal and governance structure that can adapt to the rapid advancements in AI technology.
• This proactive approach is essential for harnessing AI's potential to revolutionize healthcare in India while mitigating associated risks.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
Role of civil services in a democracy.
prelimsPoints: AI applications in healthcare include diagnostics, drug discovery, personalized medicine, and robotic surgery.
NITI Aayog released 'National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence' in 2018.
The Personal Data Protection Bill (now Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023) is relevant for data privacy.
Algorithmic bias can lead to discriminatory outcomes in healthcare if training data is unrepresentative.
Explainable AI (XAI) is a concept to make AI decisions understandable to humans.
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative potential of Artificial Intelligence in improving healthcare delivery and outcomes in India.
Analyze the major ethical concerns and regulatory challenges associated with the deployment of AI in the healthcare sector.
Evaluate the importance of a robust legal and governance framework for ensuring responsible and equitable use of AI in medicine.
Examine how issues like data privacy, algorithmic bias, and accountability can be addressed in AI-driven healthcare solutions.
Suggest a multi-stakeholder approach for developing comprehensive guidelines that balance innovation with patient safety and ethical considerations.
articles.imageDescription: A doctor interacting with a holographic display showing medical data, symbolizing AI in healthcare.
articles.trivia: The first AI system used in healthcare, MYCIN, was developed in the 1970s to diagnose blood infections.

articles.headline: Archaeological Survey of India Unearths Ancient Buddhist Monastery in Jharkhand
articles.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced the discovery of the remains of a significant ancient Buddhist monastery complex in the Netarhat plateau region of Jharkhand.
• The excavation has revealed several stupas, prayer halls, monastic cells, and artifacts dating back to the Pala period (8th-12th century CE), indicating a flourishing Buddhist presence in the region.
• This finding sheds new light on the historical spread of Buddhism in eastern India and the cultural interactions between various ancient kingdoms.
• The site's strategic location suggests it might have been an important pilgrimage or educational center along ancient trade routes.
• Further research and conservation efforts are underway to preserve the site and understand its full historical significance.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
History of India and Indian National Movement.
prelimsPoints: Pala Empire ruled parts of Bihar and Bengal from 8th to 12th century CE.
Prominent Buddhist centers during Pala period include Nalanda, Vikramshila, and Odantapuri.
ASI is an attached office of the Ministry of Culture.
Buddhism originated in India in the 6th century BCE.
Jharkhand has historical links with various ancient dynasties including Mauryas and Guptas.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of recent archaeological discoveries in re-evaluating the spread and influence of Buddhism in ancient India.
Analyze the architectural and cultural contributions of the Pala dynasty, particularly in relation to Buddhist art and monastic complexes.
Examine the role of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) in preserving India's cultural heritage and promoting historical research.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities in managing and promoting newly discovered archaeological sites for tourism and education.
Trace the historical connections of Jharkhand with major ancient Indian empires and religious movements.
articles.imageDescription: Excavated ruins of an ancient brick structure, possibly a stupa or monastery wall.
articles.trivia: The famous Nalanda University, a major Buddhist learning center, flourished under the patronage of the Pala rulers.

articles.headline: Report Warns of Accelerated Himalayan Glacier Retreat and Impact on India's Water Security
articles.explainer:
• A new report by the Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology (IITM) warns of an accelerated retreat of Himalayan glaciers, posing severe long-term threats to India's water security.
• The study highlights increased melt rates due to rising temperatures and changes in precipitation patterns, impacting major river systems like the Indus, Ganga, and Brahmaputra.
• Consequences include increased flood risks in the short term, followed by water scarcity in river basins, affecting agriculture, hydropower generation, and urban water supply.
• The report calls for urgent climate change mitigation measures and enhanced adaptive strategies, including improved water resource management and infrastructure development.
• Understanding glacier dynamics is crucial for developing effective policies to safeguard the livelihoods of millions dependent on Himalayan rivers.
gsSubTopics: Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.

prelimsPoints: Himalayan range is often called the 'Third Pole' due to its vast ice reserves.
Major Indian rivers originating from Himalayas: Ganga, Yamuna, Indus, Brahmaputra.
Black carbon deposition contributes to faster glacier melt.
The Ganga basin alone supports over 500 million people.
IITM is an autonomous institute under the Ministry of Earth Sciences.
mainsPoints: Discuss the causes and consequences of accelerated Himalayan glacier retreat on India's water resources and regional climate.
Analyze the socio-economic impacts of glacier melt on agriculture, hydropower, and livelihoods in the Himalayan region and downstream areas.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current climate change adaptation and mitigation strategies in addressing the threats posed by glacier melt.
Examine the need for transboundary cooperation in water resource management, given the shared nature of Himalayan rivers.
Suggest policy interventions and technological solutions for enhancing water security and resilience in the face of glacier retreat.
articles.imageDescription: A vast glacier in the Himalayas showing signs of melting, with a river flowing from it.
articles.trivia: The Siachen Glacier is the longest glacier in the Karakoram range and the second-longest in the world's non-polar areas.

articles.headline: Government Enhances E-Governance Initiatives for Boosting Ease of Doing Business
articles.explainer:
• The Union Government announced a series of enhancements to its e-governance platforms, aimed at further improving the 'Ease of Doing Business' in India.
• These initiatives include simplifying online application processes, integrating multiple departmental services onto single portals, and reducing compliance burdens for businesses.
• Focus areas are faster approvals for licenses and permits, digital submission of documents, and transparent tracking of application statuses.
• The reforms leverage technologies like AI and blockchain to enhance efficiency, reduce human interface, and curb corruption in government-to-business (G2B) interactions.
• This push is part of India's broader strategy to attract foreign investment, promote domestic entrepreneurship, and improve its global ranking in business environment indices.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
E-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures.
prelimsPoints: India aims to be among the top 50 in the Ease of Doing Business rankings.

UMANG (Unified Mobile Application for New-age Governance) provides access to various government services.
GEM (Government e-Marketplace) is an online platform for public procurement.
Faceless assessment schemes in taxation are part of e-governance.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of e-governance initiatives in improving the 'Ease of Doing Business' and fostering economic growth in India.
Analyze the key challenges faced in the implementation of comprehensive e-governance reforms across various government departments.
Evaluate how digital platforms and technologies can enhance transparency, accountability, and efficiency in government-to-business interactions.
Examine the role of these initiatives in attracting foreign direct investment and promoting domestic entrepreneurship.
Suggest measures to bridge the digital divide and ensure equitable access to e-governance services for all stakeholders.
articles.imageDescription: A laptop screen showing various government service icons, symbolizing e-governance.
articles.trivia: The 'Ease of Doing Business' report was formerly published by the World Bank, which discontinued it in 2021.

articles.headline: India and Bhutan Strengthen Cooperation in Hydropower and Digital Connectivity
articles.explainer:
• India and Bhutan reiterated their commitment to strengthening bilateral cooperation, focusing on new avenues in hydropower development and digital connectivity.
• Discussions included expediting ongoing hydropower projects and exploring new opportunities for sustainable energy generation, crucial for Bhutan's economy and India's energy security.
• Both nations also emphasized enhancing digital infrastructure, including fiber optic networks and digital payment systems, to boost economic integration and people-to-people connectivity.
• This partnership aligns with India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy and Bhutan's vision for sustainable development and economic diversification.
• The collaboration underscores the enduring friendship and strategic importance of the India-Bhutan relationship in the Himalayan region.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: Bhutan is a landlocked country in the eastern Himalayas.
India is Bhutan's largest trading partner.
Major hydropower projects in Bhutan developed with Indian assistance include Tala, Chhukha, and Punatsangchhu.
India provides significant economic aid to Bhutan.

articles.headline: Bharatmala Pariyojana Phase II: Progress and Economic Impact on National Highways
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Road Transport and Highways provided an update on the progress of Bharatmala Pariyojana Phase II, highlighting key achievements in national highway development.
• This phase focuses on developing economic corridors, inter-corridors, feeder routes, coastal roads, and improving connectivity to ports and border areas.
• The project aims to reduce logistics costs, enhance freight movement efficiency, and unlock economic potential in underdeveloped regions.
• Significant progress has been reported in land acquisition and construction of expressways and greenfield projects, despite challenges posed by environmental clearances and funding.
• Bharatmala Pariyojana is a cornerstone of India's infrastructure development strategy, vital for economic growth and regional integration.

Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization, of resources, growth, development and employment.

Analyze the economic impact of developing economic corridors and improving connectivity on regional development and logistics costs.

articles.headline: National Health Mission Shifts Focus Towards Prevention and Management of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs)
articles.explainer:
• The National Health Mission (NHM) announced a strategic shift to prioritize the prevention, early detection, and management of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) across India.
• This reorientation recognizes the growing burden of NCDs like diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular diseases, and cancers, which account for a significant portion of mortality and morbidity.
• Key initiatives include strengthening primary healthcare infrastructure for NCD screening, promoting healthy lifestyles, and ensuring access to affordable medicines and diagnostics.
• The program emphasizes community-level awareness campaigns and integration of NCD services with existing health programs.
• Addressing NCDs is crucial for improving public health outcomes, reducing healthcare costs, and achieving Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 3 on health and well-being.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: NHM comprises two sub-missions: National Rural Health Mission (NRHM) and National Urban Health Mission (NUHM).
NCDs are chronic diseases that do not spread from person to person.
Common NCDs include cardiovascular diseases, cancer, chronic respiratory diseases, and diabetes.
Ayushman Bharat includes Health and Wellness Centres (HWCs) for comprehensive primary healthcare, including NCD screening.
SDG 3 aims to ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages.
mainsPoints: Discuss the rising burden of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) in India and their socio-economic implications.
Analyze the strategic shift of the National Health Mission (NHM) towards NCDs and its potential impact on public health outcomes.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing NCD prevention and management programs, particularly in rural and underserved areas.
Examine the role of primary healthcare, community engagement, and multi-sectoral collaboration in combating NCDs.
Suggest policy measures to promote healthy lifestyles and ensure equitable access to NCD care, aligning with SDG 3 targets.
articles.imageDescription: A doctor explaining health charts to a patient, emphasizing preventive care.
articles.trivia: Globally, NCDs kill 41 million people each year, equivalent to 74% of all deaths.

articles.headline: India Unveils New National Cyber Security Strategy 2024 to Tackle Evolving Threats
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) unveiled India's new National Cyber Security Strategy 2024, designed to address the escalating and evolving cyber threats.
• The strategy emphasizes a multi-pronged approach covering robust cyber infrastructure, capacity building, international cooperation, and a strong legal and regulatory framework.
• Key pillars include strengthening critical information infrastructure protection, promoting cyber hygiene among citizens, and fostering indigenous cybersecurity product development.
• It also focuses on creating a skilled cybersecurity workforce and establishing rapid response mechanisms for cyber incidents.
• This comprehensive strategy aims to secure India's digital ecosystem, protect national interests, and enhance resilience against cyber warfare and cybercrime.
gsSubTopics: Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
prelimsPoints: CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team) is the nodal agency for cybersecurity incidents.
Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) refers to assets vital for national security and economy.
The Information Technology Act, 2000, is the primary law for cybercrime and e-commerce in India.
India faces threats from state-sponsored cyber-attacks, ransomware, and data breaches.
The first National Cyber Security Policy was released in 2013.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving landscape of cyber threats and their implications for India's national security and economic stability.
Analyze the key pillars and objectives of India's new National Cyber Security Strategy 2024.
Evaluate the challenges in building a robust cyber defense mechanism, including capacity building and international cooperation.
Examine the role of public-private partnerships and indigenous technology development in strengthening India's cybersecurity posture.
Suggest measures to enhance cyber awareness among citizens and establish an effective legal and regulatory framework for cybersecurity governance.

articles.trivia: The Stuxnet computer worm, discovered in 2010, was one of the first known cyberweapons used to target industrial control systems.

highlights: India launches Phase II of National Green Hydrogen Grid Mission, targets 20 GW capacity by 2030, reinforcing energy transition goals.
Supreme Court streamlines 'Living Will' guidelines for passive euthanasia, enhancing patient autonomy and dignity in end-of-life decisions.
India-EFTA Trade and Economic Partnership Agreement (TEPA) nears ratification, promising boosted trade and investment with European partners.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the National Green Hydrogen Mission, consider the following statements:
1. The mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.
2. The Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition (SIGHT) programme is a key component offering incentives for electrolyser manufacturing.
3. Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water using electricity generated from fossil fuels.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. The National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to establish India as a global hub for green hydrogen production, utilization, and export. Statement 2 is correct. The SIGHT programme is a major component of the mission, designed to provide financial incentives for domestic manufacturing of electrolysers and production of green hydrogen. Statement 3 is incorrect. Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water using electricity from renewable energy sources, not fossil fuels. If electricity from fossil fuels is used, it would be 'grey' or 'blue' hydrogen depending on carbon capture.

prelimsMocks.question:
Regarding 'Living Will' and Passive Euthanasia in India, consider the following statements:
1. The Supreme Court first recognized the 'Right to Die with Dignity' as part of Article 21 in the Common Cause vs Union of India case in 2018.
2. Passive euthanasia involves actively administering a lethal substance to end a patient's life.
3. The recent Supreme Court guidelines aim to simplify the process of executing and implementing Advance Medical Directives.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. The Supreme Court, in its landmark 2018 judgment in Common Cause (A Regd. Society) vs Union of India, recognized the 'Right to Die with Dignity' as a fundamental right under Article 21, allowing for passive euthanasia through 'Living Wills'. Statement 2 is incorrect. Passive euthanasia involves the withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment, allowing natural death. Actively administering a lethal substance is active euthanasia, which is illegal in India. Statement 3 is correct. The recent guidelines specifically aim to streamline the procedural aspects for 'Living Wills' to make them more practical and accessible.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following countries are members of the European Free Trade Association (EFTA), with which India is negotiating a Trade and Economic Partnership Agreement (TEPA)?
1. Sweden
2. Norway
3. Switzerland
4. Iceland
5. Liechtenstein
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: The European Free Trade Association (EFTA) is an intergovernmental organization established in 1960 to promote free trade and economic integration to the benefit of its four member states: Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway, and Switzerland. Sweden is a member of the European Union, not EFTA.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Ramsar Convention:
1. It is an intergovernmental treaty that provides the framework for the conservation and wise use of wetlands and their resources.
2. The 'Montreux Record' is a register of Ramsar Sites where changes in ecological character have occurred, are occurring, or are likely to occur.
3. Chilika Lake was the first Ramsar site designated in India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. The Ramsar Convention, signed in Ramsar, Iran, in 1971, is indeed an intergovernmental treaty for wetland conservation. Statement 2 is correct. The Montreux Record is maintained as part of the Ramsar List, indicating wetlands where ecological change has threatened or is threatening their character. Statement 3 is incorrect. Chilika Lake and Keoladeo National Park were designated as the first Ramsar sites in India simultaneously in 1981, not just Chilika Lake alone.

prelimsMocks.question:
With respect to the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. It aims to increase the Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in higher education to 50% by 2035.
2. It proposes a 5+3+3+4 curricular structure for school education.
3. The Academic Bank of Credits (ABC) system allows for transfer of credits earned from different higher education institutions.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. A key aim of NEP 2020 is to achieve 50% GER in higher education by 2035. Statement 2 is correct. The policy shifts from the 10+2 system to a new 5+3+3+4 curricular structure for school education. Statement 3 is correct. The Academic Bank of Credits (ABC) is a virtual/digital storehouse that contains the information of the credits earned by individual students throughout their learning journey, allowing for flexibility and transferability.

GS1.headline: Archaeological Survey of India Unearths Ancient Buddhist Monastery in Jharkhand
GS1.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced the discovery of the remains of a significant ancient Buddhist monastery complex in the Netarhat plateau region of Jharkhand.
• The excavation has revealed several stupas, prayer halls, monastic cells, and artifacts dating back to the Pala period (8th-12th century CE), indicating a flourishing Buddhist presence in the region.
• This finding sheds new light on the historical spread of Buddhism in eastern India and the cultural interactions between various ancient kingdoms.
• The site's strategic location suggests it might have been an important pilgrimage or educational center along ancient trade routes.
• Further research and conservation efforts are underway to preserve the site and understand its full historical significance.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
History of India and Indian National Movement.
prelimsPoints: Pala Empire ruled parts of Bihar and Bengal from 8th to 12th century CE.
Prominent Buddhist centers during Pala period include Nalanda, Vikramshila, and Odantapuri.
ASI is an attached office of the Ministry of Culture.
Buddhism originated in India in the 6th century BCE.
Jharkhand has historical links with various ancient dynasties including Mauryas and Guptas.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of recent archaeological discoveries in re-evaluating the spread and influence of Buddhism in ancient India.
Analyze the architectural and cultural contributions of the Pala dynasty, particularly in relation to Buddhist art and monastic complexes.
Examine the role of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) in preserving India's cultural heritage and promoting historical research.

Trace the historical connections of Jharkhand with major ancient Indian empires and religious movements.
GS1.imageDescription: Excavated ruins of an ancient brick structure, possibly a stupa or monastery wall.
GS1.trivia: The famous Nalanda University, a major Buddhist learning center, flourished under the patronage of the Pala rulers.

GS1.headline: Report Warns of Accelerated Himalayan Glacier Retreat and Impact on India's Water Security
GS1.explainer:
• A new report by the Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology (IITM) warns of an accelerated retreat of Himalayan glaciers, posing severe long-term threats to India's water security.
• The study highlights increased melt rates due to rising temperatures and changes in precipitation patterns, impacting major river systems like the Indus, Ganga, and Brahmaputra.
• Consequences include increased flood risks in the short term, followed by water scarcity in river basins, affecting agriculture, hydropower generation, and urban water supply.
• The report calls for urgent climate change mitigation measures and enhanced adaptive strategies, including improved water resource management and infrastructure development.
• Understanding glacier dynamics is crucial for developing effective policies to safeguard the livelihoods of millions dependent on Himalayan rivers.

Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: Himalayan range is often called the 'Third Pole' due to its vast ice reserves.
Major Indian rivers originating from Himalayas: Ganga, Yamuna, Indus, Brahmaputra.
Black carbon deposition contributes to faster glacier melt.
The Ganga basin alone supports over 500 million people.
IITM is an autonomous institute under the Ministry of Earth Sciences.
mainsPoints: Discuss the causes and consequences of accelerated Himalayan glacier retreat on India's water resources and regional climate.
Analyze the socio-economic impacts of glacier melt on agriculture, hydropower, and livelihoods in the Himalayan region and downstream areas.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current climate change adaptation and mitigation strategies in addressing the threats posed by glacier melt.
Examine the need for transboundary cooperation in water resource management, given the shared nature of Himalayan rivers.
Suggest policy interventions and technological solutions for enhancing water security and resilience in the face of glacier retreat.
GS1.imageDescription: A vast glacier in the Himalayas showing signs of melting, with a river flowing from it.
GS1.trivia: The Siachen Glacier is the longest glacier in the Karakoram range and the second-longest in the world's non-polar areas.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Streamlines Guidelines for 'Living Will' and Passive Euthanasia
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court issued revised guidelines to simplify the procedure for executing and implementing 'Living Wills' for passive euthanasia, addressing practical difficulties faced since its 2018 judgment.
• The new guidelines aim to make the process more accessible and less cumbersome, reducing bureaucratic hurdles and ensuring timely decisions in critical medical situations.
• Key changes include streamlining the role of medical boards, clarifying the involvement of family members, and emphasizing the patient's right to dignity in death.
• The court reiterated the importance of an Advance Medical Directive (Living Will) as an expression of an individual's right to self-determination and bodily autonomy under Article 21.
• This move is expected to provide greater clarity for both patients and healthcare providers, ensuring that end-of-life decisions are respected.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Article 21 (Right to Life) includes the right to die with dignity.
Passive euthanasia involves withdrawing life support, while active euthanasia involves administering lethal substances.

The court's ruling mandates that the 'Living Will' be attested by a notary or gazetted officer and two independent witnesses.
mainsPoints: Analyze the ethical and legal complexities surrounding the 'Right to Die with Dignity' in India.

GS2.headline: India-EFTA Trade and Economic Partnership Agreement (TEPA) Nears Ratification
GS2.explainer:
• The India-EFTA Trade and Economic Partnership Agreement (TEPA) is reportedly in its final stages of ratification, signaling a major boost for India's trade relations with European Free Trade Association (EFTA) member states.
• TEPA aims to reduce tariffs, enhance market access for goods and services, and promote investment between India and EFTA countries (Switzerland, Norway, Iceland, and Liechtenstein).
• India is expected to gain significant access to European markets for its services sector, particularly IT, while EFTA countries will benefit from reduced duties on industrial goods and technology transfer.
• The agreement includes commitments on Intellectual Property Rights (IPR), sustainable development, and dispute settlement mechanisms, fostering a comprehensive economic partnership.
• Ratification of TEPA is seen as a strategic move to diversify India's trade partners and integrate further into global supply chains amidst evolving geopolitical landscapes.

Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.

India's key imports from EFTA include machinery, pharmaceuticals, and chemicals.
The agreement includes a legally binding commitment to promote investments and job creation.

GS2.headline: National Education Policy 2020: Implementation Challenges in Higher Education Sector
GS2.explainer:

• Issues include inadequate funding, shortage of qualified faculty, resistance to curriculum reforms, and disparities in infrastructure across institutions.
• The multi-disciplinary approach and flexible credit system, while transformative, require substantial investment in faculty training and institutional capacity building.
• Stakeholder consultations reveal concerns about the pace of change and the need for greater clarity on regulatory frameworks and accreditation processes.
• Successful implementation of NEP 2020 is critical for transforming India into a global knowledge superpower and addressing skill gaps in the workforce.

GS2.headline: Government Proposes Amendments to PMLA to Broaden Scope, Include New Offenses
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Finance is reportedly working on amendments to the Prevention of Money Laundering Act (PMLA), 2002, to broaden its scope and include new categories of offenses.
• The proposed changes aim to strengthen India's legal framework against financial crimes, bring it in line with international standards set by the Financial Action Task Force (FATF), and tackle emerging forms of money laundering.
• New offenses under consideration include certain types of cyber fraud, illegal wildlife trade financing, and specific tax evasion schemes.
• The amendments also seek to enhance the powers of the Enforcement Directorate (ED) while ensuring safeguards against misuse.
• This move reflects India's commitment to combating illicit financial flows and improving its global standing in anti-money laundering efforts.

GS2.headline: India Actively Engages in COP29 Preparatory Talks, Emphasizes Climate Finance for Developing Nations
GS2.explainer:
• India is actively participating in preparatory discussions for the upcoming COP29 (Conference of the Parties) in Azerbaijan, strongly advocating for enhanced climate finance for developing countries.
• India's stance highlights the need for developed nations to meet their commitment of $100 billion annually and to establish a new, higher collective quantified goal (NCQG) for post-2025 climate finance.
• Discussions focus on adaptation finance, loss and damage mechanisms, and ensuring equitable burden-sharing in global climate action.
• India also stressed the importance of technology transfer and capacity building to support developing economies in their transition to green pathways.
• The preparatory talks are crucial for shaping the agenda and potential outcomes of COP29, particularly regarding financial support and implementation mechanisms.

GS2.headline: Government Enhances E-Governance Initiatives for Boosting Ease of Doing Business
GS2.explainer:
• The Union Government announced a series of enhancements to its e-governance platforms, aimed at further improving the 'Ease of Doing Business' in India.
• These initiatives include simplifying online application processes, integrating multiple departmental services onto single portals, and reducing compliance burdens for businesses.
• Focus areas are faster approvals for licenses and permits, digital submission of documents, and transparent tracking of application statuses.
• The reforms leverage technologies like AI and blockchain to enhance efficiency, reduce human interface, and curb corruption in government-to-business (G2B) interactions.
• This push is part of India's broader strategy to attract foreign investment, promote domestic entrepreneurship, and improve its global ranking in business environment indices.
